policy_statement: Incorporate biodiversity criteria into sustainable procurement for goods/services and Cuisines Bruxelloises food contracts to combat global ecosystem erosion.
mechanism_description: The city incorporates biodiversity criteria into its sustainable procurement strategy and food contracts to help reduce global ecosystem erosion and support biodiversity preservation.
primary_causal_pathway: Procurement choices → ↓ ecosystem degradation/land-use pressure → ↑/preserved carbon stocks + ↑ ecosystem resilience → ↓ net atmospheric forcing (and local climate risk)
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: The city uses its purchasing power (tenders, contracts, supplier set) to favor goods/services (including food) with verified biodiversity-friendly production, sourcing, and supply-chain practices.  
(2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Reduced pressure on habitats and species (less land conversion, fragmentation, pollution, and overharvesting) maintains ecosystem structure and function; intact ecosystems retain biomass and soil carbon and can continue carbon sequestration and other regulating services.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Avoided release of stored carbon and/or enhanced ongoing sequestration (mitigating emissions), with potential co-benefits for buffering climate impacts (e.g., healthier watersheds/landscapes).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is that procurement-driven reductions in ecosystem degradation help preserve/strengthen ecosystems that store carbon and regulate local climate impacts.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: City procurement standards shift market demand toward biodiversity-preserving production, leading to healthier ecosystems that preserve/enhance carbon sinks and stored carbon.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While there can be indirect mitigation/resource-efficiency effects (e.g., lower embedded emissions), the mechanism as stated is explicitly biodiversity preservation—an ecosystem-centered lever—so the dominant climate-relevant pathway runs through ecosystem integrity and carbon stock/sink preservation (Nature-Based Solutions).  
3) Edge cases applied: If biodiversity criteria are implemented mainly as low-carbon purchasing (e.g., focusing on product carbon footprints) the dominant pathway could shift toward Mitigation; if focused on reducing material throughput it could shift toward Resource Efficiency. Those are not the defining features of this canonical mechanism.  
4) Confirm primary applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that embeds biodiversity criteria in procurement aims to reduce ecosystem erosion via supply-chain choices; the common climate relevance is through ecosystem preservation and associated carbon/regulating services.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether a given policy yields primarily avoided deforestation, soil-carbon protection, fisheries protection, or urban biodiversity co-benefits requires policy-specific evidence and is deferred to policy-level coding.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Could be misclassified as Mitigation when biodiversity criteria are framed/implemented chiefly as deforestation-free or low-embedded-carbon procurement; however, under this mechanism definition the core lever is ecosystem/biodiversity preservation, best captured as Nature-Based Solutions.
key_indicators: “incorporating biodiversity criteria”; “sustainable procurement strategy for goods and services”; “Cuisines Bruxelloises food contracts”; “combat the erosion of ecosystems”
co_benefits: Reduced pollution/toxic inputs; improved soil and water quality; healthier diets/sustainable food systems; improved supply-chain transparency
instance_edge_case_notes: Climate mitigation/adaptation benefits are implied via ecosystem protection (carbon stocks/resilience) but not explicitly stated; classification hinges on procurement/contract criteria rather than broader planning or incentives.
